query: evidence regarding benefits of removing unerupted third molars in young children

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Indications for extraction of a lower third molar include recurrent episodes of pericoronitis, unrestorable caries, or caries extending into the pulp. Furthermore, a horizontal or mesioangular lower third molar causing disto-cervical caries in the lower second molar is indicated for extraction to facilitate restorative treatment in the lower second molar. In addition, extraction is also indicated in third molars with odontogenic cysts or tumors, those impeding surgery (such as in patients undergoing orthognathic surgery), or in the line of a mandibular fracture. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Lower anterior crowding is not an indication of extraction of lower third molars. Crowding of the lower anterior teeth is sometimes observed simultaneously with the eruption of lower wisdom teeth. This has led to the belief that the erupting third molars cause a mesial migration of teeth, consequently moving the incisors, resulting in crowding. [11] Several studies have investigated this hypothesis. Still, most have not found a cause-effect relationship between anterior crowding and the eruption of lower third molars. [11] Consequently, Zawawi & Melis (2014) argue that extraction of lower third molars does not alleviate anterior crowding and therefore does not justify the potential risk of damage to IAN. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This activity for healthcare professionals is designed to enhance learners' competence in identifying individuals with unerupted teeth that warrant extraction and administering the treatment properly. Participants gain deeper insights into the relevant anatomy, dental developmental patterns, and proper surgical techniques, as well as the procedure's indications, contraindications, preparation requirements, and potential complications. Greater proficiency will equip clinicians to collaborate effectively within an interprofessional team caring for patients requiring this treatment, improving outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Unerupted teeth are often asymptomatic and disease-free but still extracted prophylactically in healthy patients despite limited evidence supporting the effectiveness of this approach. [20] In cases where repositioning impacted teeth within the alveolar bone is not feasible, extraction may be the only treatment option. Factors such as age, tooth position and health, adjacent teeth, arch length, and occlusal relationship should be carefully evaluated before extraction.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Where CBCT indicates an intimate relationship between the roots of a lower third molar and the IAN, or there are other contraindications for extraction, coronectomy serves as an alternative procedure to surgical removal. Coronectomy involves the removal of the coronal tooth structure, ensuring the elimination of all enamel while the roots are left in situ. The purpose of this procedure is to remove the source of discomfort, for instance, the bulbous crown of a partially erupted lower third molar leading to food packing and subsequent pericoronitis, while the roots are left in situ, thus reducing the risk of damage to the IAN.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Some limitations of extracting unerupted teeth include systemic conditions, intake of certain medications, and tooth-related factors. Careful assessment of patient-specific factors is essential to minimize risks and complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Before extraction, the surgeon must conduct a comprehensive evaluation, including medical history, physical examination, and appropriate imaging. Patients should be informed about the procedure's risks, benefits, and alternatives and provide written consent.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Contraindications -- Bleeding Disorders and Anticoagulant Therapy Use
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who have bleeding disorders or take anticoagulants are at increased risk of excessive bleeding during dental extractions. Studies recommend continuing anticoagulant therapy for invasive procedures, as the risks associated with discontinuation outweigh the benefits of bleeding control.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Research indicates that certain factors, such as being female, having retained lower primary canines, delaying treatment, and having maxillary peg lateral incisors, can increase the severity of impaction. [21] These factors should be considered when planning preventive or interceptive procedures in young patients. If left untreated, unerupted or impacted teeth can lead to numerous complications, such as pain, infection, crown resorption, root resorption and displacement of neighboring teeth, ankylosis, and infraocclusion.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Contraindications -- Tooth Proximity to Vital Structures
retrieved_docs.content: Tooth proximity to vital structures is another limitation to the extraction of unerupted teeth. Mandibular 3rd molar roots are often too close to the IAN. The Roods criteria demonstrate the radiographic prediction of IAN injury during mandibular 3rd molar extractions. A coronectomy may be indicated as an alternative to extraction to prevent nerve damage. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Tooth impaction may affect anyone, with incidence rates varying from 5.6% to 18.8%, and it tends to be more common in female individuals. The frequency of tooth impaction follows a pattern opposite to the typical chronological eruption sequence. Teeth that erupt later are more likely to be impacted due to insufficient arch space. The mandibular 3rd molar is the most commonly impacted tooth, followed by the maxillary 3rd molar, maxillary canine, mandibular premolars, maxillary premolars, and 2nd molar. The teeth with the lowest impaction rates are the mandibular incisors, 1st molars, and primary teeth. Impaction of these teeth is relatively rare and is typically associated with specific conditions, such as retained deciduous teeth or abnormalities like an odontoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The general technique for extracting unerupted or impacted teeth can be divided into 3 main steps: Reflection of an adequately sized flap to allow access Removal of the bone surrounding the impacted tooth Luxation and removal of the tooth

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Contraindications -- Cerebrovascular Diseases
retrieved_docs.content: A recent history of transient ischemic attack increases the risk of stroke during dental procedures. A thorough medical evaluation is necessary to mitigate these risks.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Mandibular third molars can be surgically challenging to remove. Their proximity to essential structures, such as the inferior dental and the lingual nerve, is paramount when surgically planning for extraction. It is, therefore, important to consider alternative treatment modalities, such as coronectomy, where indicated. Careful clinical and radiographic assessment is crucial to successful patient outcomes when extracting mandibular third molars. Dental assistants and/or hygienists must take the appropriate radiographic views for the dentist to correctly identify the condition and the appropriate way forward.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a dental procedure that directly targets teeth (zuby) and is recognized as part of the broader set of dental procedures. Dental procedures themselves are also performed directly on deciduous teeth (zuby mléčné). Disorders of teeth and jaw are categorized under the parent concept of teeth (zuby) and are specifically located in the deciduous dentition (zuby mléčné). Consequently, both the extraction of a single tooth and other dental procedures may be applied to permanent and baby teeth, while disorders of the teeth and jaw primarily affect the baby teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2339
retrieved_docs.content: Obstetrics excision is a broad surgical category in obstetric care that encompasses several specific procedures. It includes the surgical treatment of miscarriage of any trimester, as well as the surgical treatment of missed abortion of the second trimester, missed miscarriage of the first trimester, and missed miscarriage of the third trimester. In addition, Obstetrics excision also comprises termination of an ectopic pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل خارج الرحم) and lower‑segment cesarean delivery (ولادة قيصرية في الجزء السفلي). Together, these child procedures illustrate the range of obstetric conditions that can be managed through excisional surgical techniques.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2071
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy-Related Procedures comprise a range of interventions used to manage complex obstetric conditions and facilitate safe delivery. Among these, the surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancy and abdominal pregnancy represents critical operative approaches for addressing abnormal implantation of the embryo, thereby preventing life‑threatening complications for the mother. In addition, Vaginal Assisted Delivery is included as a procedural option that supports the progression of labor and assists in the birth of the infant when additional support is required. Together, these child procedures under the umbrella of Pregnancy-Related Procedures illustrate the spectrum of surgical and assisted techniques employed in modern obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2833
retrieved_docs.content: The category “الإجراءات العلاجية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر” (Therapeutic procedures, not classified elsewhere) includes “جراحة” (Surgery) as a child concept, indicating that surgery is considered a specific type of therapeutic procedure within this broader, uncategorized therapeutic classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1313
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect site for various other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. Within this broader category, the specific intervention described as تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي (placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway) is classified as a child procedure. Recognizing that the larynx may be indirectly involved in such non‑therapeutic respiratory interventions, including the insertion of an oral‑pharyngeal airway, is important for clinicians when assessing airway management and potential procedural complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 226

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 109
retrieved_docs.content: disorders, and obstetric interventions together form a comprehensive framework for risk assessment, care planning, and management of maternal‑fetal outcomes during pregnancy, delivery, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1640
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy‑Related Procedures encompass a range of interventions that are linked to the outcome of having a child. This category includes the surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancy and abdominal pregnancy, as well as Vaginal Assisted Delivery, both of which are performed to manage complex pregnancy situations and facilitate successful childbirth. In addition, the subcategory بضع الفرج falls under Pregnancy‑Related Procedures, further illustrating the breadth of techniques aimed at ensuring a viable delivery. All of these procedures share the common purpose of supporting the continuation of pregnancy to the point of delivering a child.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2382
retrieved_docs.content: Antenatal/postnatal care encompasses the management of severe birth‑related injuries, recognizing that a Mutilerende ingreep voor bevalling (a potentially mutilating procedure during delivery) may occur and that prompt Repair of obstetric laceration is an essential component of comprehensive maternal care. By addressing both the prevention of traumatic delivery interventions and the timely surgical repair of obstetric lacerations, antenatal/postnatal care aims to protect maternal health and promote optimal recovery after childbirth.
